what: Not All Dates Are Created Equal
title: Manual
text: vignette presents different methods dating archaeological assemblages using artifact count data. , dating refers “placement time events relative one another established scale temporal measurement” (Dean 1978). involves distinguishing relative (provide chronological sequence events) absolute dating methods (yield calendric indication may provide duration event) (O’Brien Lyman 2002). Strictly speaking, absolute dating given dates produced given date refers scale. distinction absolute relative time can rephrased clearly quantifiable vs non-quantifiable (O’Brien Lyman 2002): absolute dates “expressed points standard scales time measurement” (Dean 1978). keep distinction date age formulated Colman, Pierce, Birkeland (1987): “date specific point time, whereas age interval time measured back present.” Dealing dates archaeology can tricky one take account sources chronological information. cases, date represents terminus given archaeological assemblage. , date (terminus ante-quem) (terminus post-quem) formation process assemblage took place. mind, one obvious question underlie investigation date represent? First, let’s formal: archaeological event determined unknown calendar date \(t\) associated error \(\delta t\). \(t \pm \delta t\) can provided different means assumed related event. implies : error-free dates archaeology (although uncertainties always quantified). Errors assumed symmetrical. true physical dating methods, may false data processing (e.g. 14C calibration). set \(m\) assemblages \(p\) different types artifact recorded, let \(X = \left[ x_{ij} \right] ~\forall \\left[ 1,m \right], j \\left[ 1,p \right]\) \(m \times p\) count matrix row column sums: \[ \begin{align}  x_{\cdot} = \sum_{j = 1}^{p} x_{ij} &&  x_{\cdot j} = \sum_{= 1}^{m} x_{ij} &&  x_{\cdot \cdot} = \sum_{= 1}^{m} x_{\cdot} = \sum_{j = 1}^{p} x_{\cdot j} &&  \forall x_{ij} \\mathbb{N} \end{align} \] Note \(x_{ij}\) assumed error-free.

title: Manual
text: Mean Ceramic Date (MCD) point estimate occupation archaeological site (South 1977). MCD estimated weighted mean date midpoints ceramic types \(t_j\) (based absolute dates known production interval) found given assemblage. weights conditional frequencies respective types assemblage. MCD defined : \[  t^{MCD}_i = \sum_{j = 1}^{p} t_j \times \frac{x_{ij}}{x_{\cdot}} \]

title: Manual
text: MCD point estimate: knowing mid-date assemblage knowing time span accumulation might short sighted. MCD offers rough indication chronological position assemblage, tell assemblage represents ten 100 years.

title: Manual
code: ## Coerce the zuni dataset to an abundance (count) matrix data("zuni", package = "folio") zuni_counts <- as_count(zuni)  ## Set the start and end dates for each ceramic type zuni_dates <- list(   LINO = c(600, 875), KIAT = c(850, 950), RED = c(900, 1050),    GALL = c(1025, 1125), ESC = c(1050, 1150), PUBW = c(1050, 1150),   RES = c(1000, 1200), TULA = c(1175, 1300), PINE = c(1275, 1350),   PUBR = c(1000, 1200), WING = c(1100, 1200), WIPO = c(1125, 1225),   SJ = c(1200, 1300), LSJ = c(1250, 1300), SPR = c(1250, 1300),   PINER = c(1275, 1325), HESH = c(1275, 1450), KWAK = c(1275, 1450) )  ## Calculate date midpoint zuni_mid <- vapply(X = zuni_dates, FUN = mean, FUN.VALUE = numeric(1))  ## Calculate MCD zuni_mcd <- mcd(zuni_counts, dates = zuni_mid) head(zuni_mcd) #> LZ1105 LZ1103 LZ1100 LZ1099 LZ1097 LZ1096  #>   1162   1138   1154   1091   1092    841

title: Manual
text: Event accumulation dates density estimates occupation duration archaeological site (Bellanger, Husi, Tomassone 2006; Bellanger, Tomassone, Husi 2008; Bellanger Husi 2012). event date estimation terminus post-quem archaeological assemblage. accumulation date represents “chronological profile” assemblage. According Bellanger Husi (2012), accumulation date can interpreted “best […] formation process reflecting duration succession events scale archaeological time, worst, imprecise dating due contamination context residual intrusive material.” words, accumulation dates estimate occurrence archaeological events rhythms long term.

title: Manual
text: Event dates estimated fitting Gaussian multiple linear regression model factors resulting correspondence analysis - somewhat similar idea introduced Poblome Groenen (2003). model results known dates selection reliable contexts allows predict event dates remaining assemblages. First, correspondence analysis (CA) carried summarize information count matrix \(X\). correspondence analysis \(X\) provides coordinates \(m\) rows along \(q\) factorial components, denoted \(f_{ik} ~\forall \\left[ 1,m \right], k \\left[ 1,q \right]\). , assuming \(n\) assemblages reliably dated another source, Gaussian multiple linear regression model fitted factorial components \(n\) dated assemblages: \[ t^E_i = \beta_{0} + \sum_{k = 1}^{q} \beta_{k} f_{ik} + \epsilon_i ~\forall \[1,n] \] \(t^E_i\) known date point estimate \(\)th assemblage, \(\beta_k\) regression coefficients \(\epsilon_i\) normally, identically independently distributed random variables, \(\epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)\). \(n\) equations stacked together written matrix notation \[ t^E = F \beta + \epsilon \] \(\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}_{n}(0,\sigma^2 I_{n})\), \(\beta = \left[ \beta_0 \cdots \beta_q \right]' \\mathbb{R}^{q+1}\) \[ F =  \begin{bmatrix} 1 & f_{11} & \cdots & f_{1q} \\ 1 & f_{21} & \cdots & f_{2q} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & f_{n1} & \cdots & f_{nq} \end{bmatrix} \] Assuming \(F'F\) nonsingular, ordinary least squares estimator unknown parameter vector \(\beta\) : \[ \widehat{\beta} = \left( F'F \right)^{-1} F' t^E \] Finally, given vector CA coordinates \(f_i\), predicted event date assemblage \(t^E_i\) : \[ \widehat{t^E_i} = f_i \hat{\beta} \] endpoints \(100(1 − \alpha)\)% associated prediction confidence interval given : \[ \widehat{t^E_i} \pm t_{\alpha/2,n-q-1} \sqrt{\widehat{V}} \] \(\widehat{V_i}\) estimator variance prediction error:  \[ \widehat{V_i} = \widehat{\sigma}^2 \left( f_i^T \left( F'F \right)^{-1} f_i + 1 \right) \] \(\widehat{\sigma} = \frac{\sum_{=1}^{n} \left( t_i - \widehat{t^E_i} \right)^2}{n - q - 1}\). probability density event date \(t^E_i\) can described normal distribution: \[ t^E_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\widehat{t^E_i},\widehat{V_i}) \]

title: Manual
text: row (assemblages) columns (types) CA coordinates linked together -called transition formulae, event dates type \(t^E_j\) can predicted following procedure . , accumulation date \(t^A_i\) defined weighted mean event date ceramic types found given assemblage. weights conditional frequencies respective types assemblage (akin MCD). accumulation date estimated : \[  \widehat{t^A_i} = \sum_{j = 1}^{p} \widehat{t^E_j} \times \frac{x_{ij}}{x_{\cdot}} \] probability density accumulation date \(t^A_i\) can described Gaussian mixture: \[ t^A_i \sim \frac{x_{ij}}{x_{\cdot}} \mathcal{N}(\widehat{t^E_j},\widehat{V_j}^2) \] Interestingly, integral accumulation date offers estimates cumulative occurrence archaeological events, close enough definition tempo plot introduced Dye (2016).

title: Manual
text: package provides implementation chronological modeling method developed Bellanger Husi (2012). method slightly modified allows construction different probability density curves archaeological assemblage dates (event, activity tempo).   Resampling methods can used check stability resulting model. jackknife() used, one type/fabric removed time statistics recalculated. way, one can assess whether certain type/fabric substantial influence date estimate. bootstrap() used, large number new bootstrap assemblages created, sample size, resampling original assemblage replacement. , examination bootstrap statistics makes possible pinpoint assemblages require investigation.
code: ## Bellanger et al. did not publish the data supporting their demonstration:  ## no replication of their results is possible.  ## Here is a pseudo-reproduction using the zuni dataset  ## Assume that some assemblages are reliably dated (this is NOT a real example) ## The names of the vector entries must match the names of the assemblages zuni_dates <- c(   LZ0569 = 1097, LZ0279 = 1119, CS16 = 1328, LZ0066 = 1111,   LZ0852 = 1216, LZ1209 = 1251, CS144 = 1262, LZ0563 = 1206,   LZ0329 = 1076, LZ0005Q = 859, LZ0322 = 1109, LZ0067 = 863,   LZ0578 = 1180, LZ0227 = 1104, LZ0610 = 1074 )  ## Model the event and accumulation date for each assemblage model <- event(zuni_counts, dates = zuni_dates, cutoff = 90) summary(get_model(model)) #>  #> Call: #> stats::lm(formula = date ~ ., data = contexts, na.action = stats::na.omit) #>  #> Residuals: #>         1         2         3         4         5         6         7         8  #>  0.517235 -4.017534 -0.279200  0.662137 -1.246499  0.576044  2.634482 -4.383683  #>         9        10        11        12        13        14        15  #> -1.093837 -0.005002  2.543773 -0.032706  3.480918 -0.759429  1.403301  #>  #> Coefficients: #>             Estimate Std. Error  t value Pr(>|t|)     #> (Intercept) 1164.350      1.892  615.459 2.15e-13 *** #> F1          -158.314      1.472 -107.582 1.32e-09 *** #> F2            25.629      1.444   17.753 1.04e-05 *** #> F3            -5.546      1.905   -2.912   0.0333 *   #> F4            11.416      3.407    3.351   0.0203 *   #> F5            -2.713      2.448   -1.108   0.3183     #> F6             2.697      1.181    2.285   0.0711 .   #> F7             3.966      3.001    1.322   0.2435     #> F8            11.132      2.941    3.785   0.0128 *   #> F9            -4.886      2.020   -2.418   0.0602 .   #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Residual standard error: 3.669 on 5 degrees of freedom #>   (405 observations deleted due to missingness) #> Multiple R-squared:  0.9997, Adjusted R-squared:  0.9992  #> F-statistic:  1979 on 9 and 5 DF,  p-value: 2.456e-08  ## Estimate event dates event <- predict_event(model, margin = 1, level = 0.95) head(event) #>        date lower upper error #> LZ1105 1168  1158  1178     4 #> LZ1103 1143  1139  1147     1 #> LZ1100 1156  1148  1164     3 #> LZ1099 1099  1092  1106     3 #> LZ1097 1088  1080  1097     3 #> LZ1096  839   829   849     4  ## Estimate accumulation dates acc <- predict_accumulation(model) head(acc) #> LZ1105 LZ1103 LZ1100 LZ1099 LZ1097 LZ1096  #>   1170   1140   1158   1087   1092    875 ## Activity plot plot(model, type = "activity", event = TRUE, select = "LZ1105") ## Tempo plot plot(model, type = "tempo", select = "LZ1105") ## Check model variability ## Warning: this may take a few seconds  ## Jackknife fabrics jack <- jackknife(model) head(jack) #>        date lower upper error  bias #> LZ1105 1457  1447  1466     4  4913 #> LZ1103  948   945   952     1 -3315 #> LZ1100 1094  1086  1102     3 -1054 #> LZ1099 1253  1246  1260     3  2618 #> LZ1097  917   908   925     3 -2907 #> LZ1096 1060  1050  1070     4  3757  ## Bootstrap of assemblages boot <- bootstrap(model, n = 30) head(boot) #>         min     mean  max      Q5     Q95 #> LZ1105 1124 1166.867 1193 1128.00 1190.65 #> LZ1103 1085 1142.100 1207 1098.50 1197.10 #> LZ1100 1076 1153.533 1225 1090.95 1203.55 #> LZ1099 1091 1099.133 1108 1092.90 1105.55 #> LZ1097  970 1096.133 1194 1029.50 1174.20 #> LZ1096  726  862.100 1123  726.00  983.35

Birkeland. 1987. “Suggested Terminology Quaternary Dating Methods.” Quaternary Research 28 (2): 314–19. https://doi.org/10.1016/0033-5894(87)90070-6. Dye, Thomas S. 2016. “Long-Term Rhythms Development Hawaiian Social Stratification.” Journal Archaeological Science 71 (July): 1–9. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jas.2016.05.006. O’Brien, Michael J, R. Lee Lyman. 2002. Seriation, Stratigraphy, Index Fossils: Backbone Archaeological Dating. Dordrecht: Springer. Poblome, J., P. J. F. Groenen. 2003. “Constrained Correspondence Analysis Seriation Sagalassos Tablewares.” Digital Heritage Archaeology, edited M. Doerr . Sarris. Hellenic Ministry Culture. South, S. . 1977. title: Analysis of Chronological Patterns from Archaeological Count Data
text: toolkit absolute dating analysis chronological patterns. package includes functions chronological modeling dating archaeological assemblages count data. allows compute time point estimates density estimates occupation duration archaeological site. kairos provides methods : Mean ceramic date estimation (South 1977): mcd() Event accumulation date estimation (Bellanger Husi 2012): event() Aoristic analysis (Ratcliffe 2000): aoristic() Chronological apportioning (Roberts et al. 2012): apportion()

title: Analysis of Chronological Patterns from Archaeological Count Data
text: can install released version kairos CRAN : development version GitHub :

title: Analysis of Chronological Patterns from Archaeological Count Data
text: kairos supports dates expressed CE years (BCE years must given negative numbers). results rounded zero decimal places (sub-annual precision make sense situations). may affect results calculations, especially resampling methods. can change behavior options(kairos.precision = 2) (two decimal places). kairos uses set S4 classes represent different special types matrix. Please refer documentation arkhe package classes defined. assumes keep data tidy: variable (type/taxa) must saved column observation (sample/case) must saved row.
code: ## Load packages library(kairos) library(folio) # datasets ## Aoristic Analysis data("zuni", package = "folio")  ## Set the start and end dates for each ceramic type dates <- list(   LINO = c(600, 875), KIAT = c(850, 950), RED = c(900, 1050),   GALL = c(1025, 1125), ESC = c(1050, 1150), PUBW = c(1050, 1150),   RES = c(1000, 1200), TULA = c(1175, 1300), PINE = c(1275, 1350),   PUBR = c(1000, 1200), WING = c(1100, 1200), WIPO = c(1125, 1225),   SJ = c(1200, 1300), LSJ = c(1250, 1300), SPR = c(1250, 1300),   PINER = c(1275, 1325), HESH = c(1275, 1450), KWAK = c(1275, 1450) )  ## Keep only assemblages that have a sample size of at least 10 keep <- apply(X = zuni, MARGIN = 1, FUN = function(x) sum(x) >= 10)  ## Calculate date ranges for each assemblage span <- apply(   X = zuni[keep, ],   FUN = function(x, dates) range(unlist(dates[x > 0])),   MARGIN = 1,   dates = dates )  ## Coerce to data.frame span <- as.data.frame(t(span)) colnames(span) <- c("from", "to")  ## Calculate aoristic sum (weights) aorist_weigth <- aoristic(span, step = 50, weight = TRUE) plot(aorist_weigth) ## Rate of change set.seed(12345) aorist_roc <- roc(aorist_weigth, n = 30) plot(aorist_roc)

title: Aoristic Analysis — aoristic
code: aoristic(x, y, ...)  # S4 method for numeric,numeric aoristic(   x,   y,   step = 1,   start = min(x, na.rm = TRUE),   stop = max(y, na.rm = TRUE),   weight = TRUE,   groups = NULL )  # S4 method for list,missing aoristic(x, step = 1, start = NULL, stop = NULL, weight = FALSE, groups = NULL)

title: Aoristic Analysis — aoristic
text: x numeric vector. y missing, must list (data.frame) numeric components (columns) . y numeric vector. missing, attempt made interpret x suitable way. ... Currently used. step length-one integer vector giving step size, .e. width time step time series (years CE; defaults \(1\) - .e. annual level). start length-one numeric vector giving beginning time window (years CE). stop length-one numeric vector giving end time window (years CE). weight logical scalar: aoristic sum weighted length periods. Defaults FALSE (aoristic sum number elements within time step). groups factor vector sense .factor(groups) defines grouping. x list (data.frame), groups can length-one vector giving index grouping component (column) x.

title: Aoristic Analysis — aoristic
text: AoristicSum object.

title: Aoristic Analysis — aoristic
text: Aoristic analysis used determine probability contemporaneity archaeological sites assemblages. aoristic analysis distributes probability event uniformly temporal fraction period considered. aoristic sum distribution total number events assumed within period. Muller Hinz (2018) pointed overlapping temporal intervals related period categorization dating accuracy likely bias analysis. proposed weighting method overcome problem. method implemented (moment), see aoristAAR package.

title: Chronological Apportioning — apportion
text: object \(m \times p\) arkhe::CountMatrix object. ... Currently used. s0 length-\(m\) numeric vector giving site beginning dates (years CE). s1 length-\(m\) numeric vector giving site end dates (years CE). t0 length-\(p\) numeric vector giving type beginning dates (years CE). t1 length-\(p\) numeric vector giving type end dates (years CE). length-one numeric vector giving beginning period interest (years CE). length-one numeric vector giving end period interest (years CE). step length-one integer vector giving step size, .e. width time step apportioning (years CE; defaults \(25\)). method character string specifying distribution used (type popularity curve). must one "uniform" (uniform distribution) "truncated" (truncated standard normal distribution). unambiguous substring can given. z integer value giving lower upper truncation points (defaults \(2\)). used method "truncated". progress logical scalar: progress bar displayed?

title: Event and Accumulation Dates — event
code: event(object, dates, ...)  predict_event(object, data, ...)  predict_accumulation(object, data, ...)  # S4 method for CountMatrix,numeric event(object, dates, cutoff = 90, level = 0.95, ...)  # S4 method for EventDate,missing predict_event(object, margin = 1, level = 0.95)  # S4 method for EventDate,CountMatrix predict_event(object, data, margin = 1, level = 0.95)  # S4 method for EventDate,missing predict_accumulation(object)  # S4 method for EventDate,CountMatrix predict_accumulation(object, data)  # S4 method for EventDate jackknife(object, level = 0.95, progress = getOption("kairos.progress"), ...)  # S4 method for EventDate bootstrap(   object,   level = 0.95,   probs = c(0.05, 0.95),   n = 1000,   progress = getOption("kairos.progress"),   ... )

title: Event and Accumulation Dates — event
text: object arkhe::CountMatrix EventDate object. dates numeric vector dates. named, names must match row names object. ... arguments passed internal methods. data arkhe::CountMatrix object predict event accumulation dates. cutoff integer giving cumulative percentage variance used select CA factorial components linear model fitting (see details). compounds cumulative percentage variance less cutoff value retained. level length-one numeric vector giving confidence level. margin numeric vector giving subscripts prediction applied : 1 indicates rows, 2 indicates columns. progress logical scalar: progress bar displayed? probs numeric vector probabilities values \([0,1]\) (see stats::quantile()). NULL, quantiles computed. n non-negative integer giving number bootstrap replications.

title: Event and Accumulation Dates — event
text: event() returns EventDate object. predict_event() returns data.frame. predict_accumulation() returns MeanDate object. bootstrap() jackknife() return data.frame.

title: Event and Accumulation Dates — event
text: implementation chronological modeling method proposed Bellanger Husi (2012, 2013). Event accumulation dates density estimates occupation duration archaeological site (Bellanger Husi 2012, 2013). event date estimation terminus post-quem archaeological assemblage. accumulation date represents "chronological profile" assemblage. According Bellanger Husi (2012), accumulation date can interpreted "best [...] formation process reflecting duration succession events scale archaeological time, worst, imprecise dating due contamination context residual intrusive material." words, accumulation dates estimate occurrence archaeological events rhythms long term. method relies strong archaeological statistical assumptions (see vignette("kairos")).

title: Event and Accumulation Dates — event
text: Bellanger et al. publish data supporting demonstration: replication results possible. implementation must considered experimental subject major changes future release.

title: Event and Accumulation Dates — event
text: jackknife() used, one type/fabric removed time statistics recalculated. way, one can assess whether certain type/fabric substantial influence date estimate. three columns data.frame returned, giving results resampling procedure (jackknifing fabrics) assemblage (rows) following columns: mean jackknife mean (event date). bias jackknife estimate bias. error standard error predicted means. bootstrap() used, large number new bootstrap assemblages created, sample size, resampling original assemblage replacement. , examination bootstrap statistics makes possible pinpoint assemblages require investigation. five columns data.frame returned, giving bootstrap distribution statistics replicated assemblage (rows) following columns: min Minimum value. mean Mean value (event date). max Maximum value. Q5 Sample quantile 0.05 probability. Q95 Sample quantile 0.95 probability.

NULL, confidence interval computed. type character string giving type confidence interval returned. must one "student" (default) "normal". unambiguous substring can given. used level NULL.`` probs numeric vector probabilities values \([0,1]\) (see stats::quantile()). NULL, quantiles computed. n non-negative integer giving number bootstrap replications.

title: Event Plot — plot_event

Activity plot activity plot displays first derivative tempo plot.
